notes: Chapter argument reconstruction: 'God's Law Immutable' builds a single cumulative case that the Decalogue (with the seventh-day Sabbath at its center) is eternal and universally binding, and it does this in four linked moves: (1) the heavenly sanctuary opened in 1844 contains the law, tying the Millerite/investigative-judgment doctrine to the Sabbath; (2) Revelation 14's three angels command Creator-worship, which the chapter equates with fourth-commandment obedience via the Sabbath's role as creation-memorial; (3) the beast/image/two-horned-beast prophetic scheme (dragon=Rome/Satan, leopard-beast=papacy, lamb-horned beast=USA) is used to read Daniel 7 and Revelation 13 as predicting a future US-enforced 'image to the beast' via Sunday law; (4) the papacy's historical claim to have altered the Sabbath to Sunday is presented as proof that Sunday-keeping constitutes homage to Rome, setting up the mark-of-the-beast/seal-of-God dichotomy. The chapter's own argumentative weight rests less on any single historical date than on the claim that Rome asserts authority over the Sabbath-Sunday change (independently well attested via Segur/Tuberville/catechism quotes, all verified supported) and on the Edenic/creation grounding of the Sabbath's universality (GC-25-051), which is what lets the chapter treat the Sabbath as binding on all humanity rather than as Jewish ceremonial law. The 538-1798 historicist date scheme and the 'papacy first resorted to state power for Sunday' claim are inherited support beams from earlier chapters and from Andrews/Dowling-style 19th-century Sabbatarian historiography, not claims this chapter itself establishes from scratch — so their removal dents precision and rhetorical force but does not collapse this chapter's central Sabbath-perpetuity argument, which has redundant supported anchors elsewhere in the same chapter. Scoring notes: 4 items scored (3 confirmed anachronistic discrepancies + 1 high-stakes disputed item per orchestrator instruction); all four sit in the Sabbath/Sunday-change and prophetic-chronology cluster and are scored centrality 5 as the load-bearing doctrinal hinge of the book's final-crisis chain, per CLAUDE.md's centrality metric (book-level, not merely chapter-level). Severity for the three anachronistic items is kept at 3 (not 4-5) per CLAUDE.md's instruction that anachronistic errors reflecting period-typical scholarship (Andrews, Uriah Smith, Dowling) are a matter of authorial method, not invention — none of the three fabricates an event or quote; each takes a real event/quote and misdates or over-attributes intentionality to it. GC-25-051 (disputed) is scored at lower severity (2) than the anachronistic items, reflecting that it is a live, respectable scholarly disagreement (conservative canonical reading vs. Priestly-source dating) rather than a confirmed error, even though its centrality is maximal and it is the one item judged load-bearing for this chapter's specific universalizing argument.

GC-25-051.reasoning: Paragraph 11 explicitly grounds the Sabbath's status as a universal, eternal 'memorial of creation' in its institution 'in Eden' at creation, not at Sinai; this is precisely what lets the chapter later treat Sabbath-keeping as binding on all mankind (Gentile and Jew alike) rather than as a piece of the Mosaic covenant that ended with it. Critical OT scholarship dates the Genesis 1 creation-Sabbath framing to the Priestly source (post-exilic) and treats it as an etiology for the Mosaic/Israelite institution rather than evidence of Edenic observance, which would (if correct) reduce the Sabbath to a covenant-specific sign rather than a creation ordinance for all humanity. Counterfactual: delete this claim and the chapter still has other rhetorical resources (the 'Sabbath is a sign' argument, Christ's 'Son of man is Lord of the Sabbath'), but it loses its most direct textual basis for universalizing the fourth commandment beyond Israel, weakening — without destroying — the leap from 'Sabbath as Israel's sign' to 'Sabbath as the end-time test for all nations.' Scored as disputed, not contradicted, since this is genuine, ongoing historiographical disagreement rather than a settled academic refutation.

GC-25-070.reasoning: The 538-1798 = 1260-year 'papal supremacy' span is a 19th-century historicist calculation (Andrews, Uriah Smith) working backward from 1798, not an independently attested modern-historiographical dating; 538 marks a low point, not the onset, of papal power by mainstream accounts. In THIS chapter the scheme is invoked ('as stated in preceding chapters') to synchronize Daniel 7's 'time, times, and dividing of time' with Revelation 13's forty-two months and then to anchor the identification of the lamb-horned beast (USA) as 'coming up' in 1798 — but the chapter's own beast=USA argument leans on 1798 as a real, independently verified event (Pius VI's capture, GC-25-071, supported) and on the US's real rise to prominence that year (GC-25-077, supported), not on the 538 start-date specifically. Counterfactual: delete this claim and the chapter's US-identification argument and Sabbath-immutability argument both survive on their own independently supported anchors; what is lost is the precise chronological scaffolding connecting this chapter's prophecy back to the book's earlier Reformation-era chapters, which is a real loss for the book's overall interlocking chronology (hence centrality 5) but not a collapse of this chapter's argument (hence load_bearing false here, contrasted with ch. 15 where the scheme is directly argued and defended).

GC-25-126.reasoning: The claim conflates two different things: the Augustinian numbering tradition (which folds the images-prohibition into the first commandment, a numbering convention shared by Catholics and Lutherans, not a 'dropping' of scriptural text) and a genuinely attested Catholic claim of ecclesiastical authority over the Sunday-Sabbath change (which Catholic catechisms and apologetics — Segur, Tuberville — do affirm in their own words, verified supported at GC-25-142/143/149). The 'papacy has attempted to change the law of God' framing as a deliberate corrupting act is period Sabbatarian polemic (Andrews, Dowling) rather than how mainstream church history frames the numbering tradition or the gradual, centuries-long shift to Sunday (Bacchiocchi, academic-full). Counterfactual: delete this claim and the chapter's mark-of-the-beast argument is undamaged, because paragraphs 40-43 independently quote the Catholic Church's own self-description of having 'substituted Sunday for Saturday' by its own authority — the intentionalist 'dropped the second commandment' framing is rhetorically vivid but redundant with material that survives verification untouched. Centrality is maximal because this sentence sits at the exact hinge connecting Daniel 7:25 ('think to change the times and the law') to the Sabbath-Sunday identification that powers the entire end-time mark-of-the-beast doctrine across chs. 34-42.

GC-25-131.reasoning: The sentence's first half (Rome championed Sunday observance) has genuine academic support (Bacchiocchi); its second half ('its first resort to the power of the state was to compel the observance of Sunday') misattributes to papal action what modern scholarship credits to Constantine's civil edict of 321 (a pagan-imperial law, not an act of papal state power) and to a church council (Laodicea, c. 360). The sentence's function in the chapter is to draw a rhetorical parallel between the papacy's alleged historical precedent of using state coercion for Sunday and the predicted future US enforcement of Sunday law (paragraphs 148-155). Counterfactual: delete this claim and the parallel-drawing loses one supporting example, but the predictive argument does not depend on papal priority in state enforcement — it depends on Rome's claimed authority over the Sabbath-Sunday change (independently supported) and on the contemporary 1880s Sunday-law movement (Blair Bill, verified supported at GC-25-148), both of which stand untouched. Centrality is maximal because the sentence belongs to the same Sabbath/Sunday-change hinge as GC-25-126, load-bearing for the book's overall final-crisis chain even though not for this chapter's specific paragraph-level argument.
